Hello again, this is Nate and as promised from the earlier one, this is kind of my take on Chains of Belief which is part two of Andre's Invisible Influence. And so yeah, this will give you some examples and some steps to follow. So before I get into this and these crazy looking sunglasses, you know, for me to go through this stuff, it helps me quite a bit. So I hope that me going through and breaking down all of these ideas just to make sure that I understand them better. Again, I hope that this is helpful for you guys as well. So I thought it would be fun to go through kind of a crazy example of how this same chain of beliefs can work to sell virtually anything. So some of you may know who this guy is, but this guy right here is actually Joe Sugerman and he sold a billion dollars worth of these sunglasses. They were called Blue Blocker sunglasses and he did this in the 1980s. What is amazing about this is literally the birth of the infomercial time was right about this particular time. These glasses were so insanely popular. So if you were alive in the US during the 1980s, you absolutely remember some of the Blue Blocker commercials. And if you were either not alive in the 1980s or you were not alive in the US during the 1980s, you should definitely go to YouTube and look at some of the videos. It was literally like a cult sensation. We can think this advertising genius, Joe Sugerman, and the sales letter that I've included for this to go through. But I think it's a great example of how you can sequence beliefs together and you could sell literally anything. And so I have included the sales letter. For the sake of time, I'm not going to read it. It's actually very short, but I won't read it, but I'll break down kind of how we could use the chain of beliefs to understand how Joe Sugerman wrote that ad in order to sell these sunglasses. So I will include that sales letter somewhere within this post. So if we go back real quick, there's an interview also that Joe Sugerman gave to Joe Polish. And if you go to YouTube and look at Genius Network, just type in Genius Network and then Joe Sugerman and that interview will come up. And he talks about how he deliberately made specific choices while he was developing the marketing for this. And so before we kind of break down this chain of beliefs, there's a couple of critical things that we have to first kind of understand. And one of them is that he realized his big curiosity angle for this entire promotion was going to be that essentially you're not going to believe how clear you see when you put these glasses on. That was the fundamental promise of the entire ad. And so here you see up here, when I put on this pair of glasses, what I saw I could not believe, nor will you. Now what's interesting about that is that headline today would be very much synonymous with clickbait, but back then it worked just fine. But that was his big curiosity angle. He sold these sunglasses in print as well as TV commercials. And he consciously knew, he specifically made the decision to not show you in the commercial what it looked like, but he showed you the reaction of people that put on the glasses. And that was literally a billion dollar good decision that could have been a billion dollar mistake if he showed you what it looked like. So he kept curiosity very high. Okay, so just kind of some few insights. Again, you can go to Genius Network, go to YouTube, type in Genius Network, Joe Sugarman, and an interview where he talks about this and a whole bunch of other really good stuff that he did will come up and it will be well worth your time to kind of understand all of this. But let's jump into this specific case. Okay, so if we were just to map out what the chains of belief would be if I wanted to sell these sunglasses. Well if we look here at kind of the destination. So this is the final point that we need the prospect to be at when they're given the decision to buy. And so essentially I wrote it this way, if you want light, sturdy glasses that truly protect your eyes, blue blocker is the only choice. So by the time the order button shows up, that's where I want people to be. I want them to believe that. So then we might say, okay, so what do we have to have them believe before we get to that point? I need to demonstrate to them that blue blockers are sturdy. And he does this when you read the sales letter, he'll talk about the hinge is the weakest point of any particular pair of sunglasses. I've taken the following steps to address it and it's very smart. Then before that, I might need them to believe that regular glasses actually hurt your eyes. This part of the sales letter you've got to read. It is just absolutely magnificent how he talks about, and this was all high technology at the time. So he talked about harmful effects of UV rays and he compares that to what UV does to your skin. So imagine what it does to your eyes. That's brilliant. He talks about the damaging effect of your natural tendency for your pupils to dilate. That was classic. I love it. It cracks me up now even thinking about it. Then he talks about how all of these effects and damage your night vision. And he talks about how this is even made even worse when you wear contact lenses or when you wear regular glasses that do not block the harmful UV rays. So you definitely have to read that part. And then what we might also need them to believe is that blue blockers make everything sharper. And they do that because they filter blue light. And he has this idea of where the shifting focus of blue light is. And he says that blue light actually, the focal point for blue light is a little bit farther in front of your eye. And so that actually distorts the crispness of what an image could have. When blue is eliminated, it actually makes the image appear sharper. Just fabulous little proof points, which brings me up to kind of one thing that I want you to notice within the ad is anytime he makes one of these assertions, he backs it up with proof. And notice how he did it. The very first part was through a story. And then he starts listing out other facts after that that help imply or straight out say, hey, this is the science behind why something is the way it is. Okay, so we have our destination and we have a few basic beliefs that someone would have to have in order to end up believing that blue blockers are light sturdy glasses that truly protect your eyes. And a blue blocker really is the only choice. Okay, so let's break that down and look at how he told it. So here's kind of the simple logic diagram of how the whole sales letter might be mapped out within this kind of framework. So his main assertion or the main thing that he said is that these glasses will make everything more clear. And he has this story. It's great. And so he introduces this guy named Len, which I think is a classic play on the word of lens, which we're talking about sunglasses. But he describes Len as being a friend that is known for finding quality products. What I think is funny about that is if we don't know Len and we don't really know Joe Sugarman wrote the ad in the first person and we don't know Joe Sugarman by the time we read the ad, when we hear a supposed fact or reported fact about someone, do we ever question that? If I was to tell you a story and said, hi, my name's Nate and I have this friend, Mary, and Mary is blank. You're never going to doubt that. I've given you no reason to doubt that I am misreporting the way that Mary is. And so in the very beginning when he says, hi, you know, I just went through this amazing experience and it was brought to me by this, a friend of mine named Len, who is known for finding quality products. It's a classic frame right off the bat that says Len only finds good stuff. And he just introduced me to the most amazing glasses I've ever looked through. And he told me that everything would be sharp and more clear. And when I put them on, they were sharp and more clear. Let me tell you what I found out why that is. That's the basic structure of the ad. He does the story, literally takes two paragraphs. And then the rest of the ad is the science behind why they work. Okay, so let's break down. So that's the story part. So that's kind of the story structure. Let's break down the chain of beliefs. And so again, you know, earlier when back over here, we were working this way. And so now we're going to go through and we're going to tell the story and tell our facts the opposite way. So here we say, blue light is actually bad for us. That's the first belief that he introduces. And you can read the sales letter and see how he did that. Then he has this idea that that blue light focuses poorly on our eyes. And he has this little example where the focal point for blue light is actually away from the eye. There's that little gap and this is blue light. But in reality, when you put the sunglasses on, right, so we have our little lens here, when we put the sunglasses on, it actually moves this focal point to right on your eye. So things become more clear. And so there's a little, amazing little explanation as to why blue light actually prevents us from seeing things more clearly. Number three, the next belief that he has there is regular glasses actually are bad for us. This is great. This is where I talk about before he has these, I talk about the harmful UV rays and that your pupils dilate and that it's damaged to your night vision and the damaging effect of contact lenses. This is just masterful stuff. And so in this case, what we see that he's done is he's made his problem bucket, right, his problem bucket. And the problem bucket is basically regular glasses and contacts. That's the problem. And they all have these fundamental flaws. They don't block UV light, which is bad for our skin and bad for our eyes. They don't block blue light, which not only does it make things less clear, which is inherently dangerous, but it's also physically dangerous because when you put sunglasses on, it forces your pupils to dilate because there's less light overall. Well that means that you're just letting more of those harmful rays in your eye. I love it. That's just, that's classic. So then the solution bucket of course becomes the blue blocker sunglasses themselves. And then he goes on and says, blue blockers are high quality. They have the hinge. He solves the hinge problem. He has scratch proof lenses and they're as comfortable and he compares them to $200 pair of sunglasses from France and Italy. So we see kind of how we move through each one of these little beliefs and how they build on the next until by the final point when we essentially reach the decision to buy, they're really, I mean, if you want glasses that make everything more clear and don't hurt your eyes, well, the only logical choice is blue blockers. Okay. So a couple of resources. I got the sales letter that I included from Swiped.co. It's a great website where you can go and start building some of your own swipe file and look at winning ads and that's Swiped.co. We're going to give those guys credit. Joe Sugarman is the man that I talked about and he wrote a book called Triggers, which is great. You can get it from Amazon. He's also known for really exploring and using involvement devices in print ads. So if you want to look up involvement devices and Joe Sugarman, I would encourage you to do so because that really is a unique insight into things that will be discussed later, talking about kind of how you set up influential environments. Joe Sugarman was very good at writing ads that didn't necessarily look like ads. You can, I mentioned the Genius Network YouTube video that you should go check out where he talks about some of the inside story about how he developed the ad for Blue Blocker and all that good stuff. And you can also just go into a Google search for Joe Sugarman involvement devices. If you really kind of geek out on this kind of stuff like I do, you will find it extremely enjoyable. And if you don't necessarily, then you will find it at a minimum very profitable. So you should go check it out. Okay. So then I thought, all right, it would be good to kind of look at this same structure and then how I went through and kind of broke out what the chains of belief would be within the Frank versus Matt story. And so step one was just list out some of the big beliefs. What I found helpful was at this point, not getting involved in the actual story elements that I might use or the story scenes that I might use, but just what are the big intellectual chunks? You know, just what are the big beliefs? What are the major statements that I need them to agree with as they move through my story or my sales letter or my email sequence or whatever it is? You know, we can create the story later. This is just going to be a skeleton for how we kind of put all those pieces together. So in this case, so by the time the prospect sees the buy button, they should believe. Okay. That's kind of the question that I would ask myself by the time they get to the sales letter, by the time they're essentially ready to make a sales decision, a buy decision, then what is it that they need to believe? And in this case, I said that to succeed online, I need to be like Matt. That's what they need to agree with. And on the side of Frank, they need to agree with the statement that says, I am not yet successful online because I have been acting like Frank. Okay. So prior to that, what's something that they would need to agree to? Well, Matt is successful because he focuses on one thing and makes steady progress. Over here, Frank is running or excuse me, Frank is ruining any chance of success because he is constantly chasing shiny objects. But prior to that, it would be Matt avoids, I say GRQ, but get rich quick things and focuses on simple proven formulas. Over here, Frank thinks he can beat the system and is always looking for a loophole, a back door or a hack. So if we, so notice that I don't necessarily here break down the actual ways that we imply those things or the way that Andre implies those things in the story. I'd encourage you to go back through, read the story and look at where these major intellectual milestones, these major beliefs are addressed and how he uses facts and how he describes situations that makes you believe, yes, I took the same action that Frank did and he described it to a T and I got the same result Frank did. And so that's the beauty of kind of how it's structured in this particular story format. But the big thing now is just list out the beliefs. Don't worry about the ways that you'll support the beliefs necessarily. Don't worry about the ways that you're going to actually construct the story. At this point, just get these beliefs mapped out. So now what you want to do is the next step would be how you sequence the beliefs. So if you notice before, this is kind of a little symbol for step one and we worked down the page and we had kind of our big idea, which is, you know, Matt is successful because he does blank and I'm not yet successful because I'm acting like Matt or like Frank, excuse me. And then we had our other little things there. Well, now what we want to do is we want to sequence these things out. Okay. We want to have these ideas and we want to lay them out in the order in which we will present them. So we have our story structure and over here I have number one, Matt avoids get rich quick streams and he focuses on a proven system. Okay. And that was exactly the same that it was here. Matt avoids get rich quick things and focuses on simple proven formulas. And number two, we have, he makes progress because he avoids all the hype and distractions and then to be successful, I need to be like Matt. Okay. So one, two and three, then we map out Frank. Frank tries to beat the system. He ruins his success because he, uh, chase his shiny objects and I am not successful in line because I have been acting like Frank. Now at this point, at point number two right here is where you can begin to think about, okay, what's the problem bucket that I have? What's the problem that I need to, um, that I need to start identifying? And in that one, I just called it here shiny object syndrome that really, um, you know, Frank is trying all of these things. He's spinning his wheels and all of those things are a symptom of a deeper problem. And I just called for the sake of this example, shiny object syndrome. I actually can't remember what Andre called in the sales letter. It's been a while since I've read it. Um, and then, and so in this case, um, I have here that all these things are, um, a symptom of a deeper issue. And in this case, the issue name is shiny object syndrome. So we've actually already gone ahead and done that. So if you are constantly trying to beat the system, if you are bouncing from business idea to business idea, if you constantly think that one of the things that you've been told a month later is no longer true or no longer works, then essentially you're just bouncing around from, from top of the topic and you're suffering from what we call shiny object syndrome is essentially how that would be. So by the time that you have gone through step two where you sequence out the beliefs, you essentially should have, right, you should have your story structure kind of laid out, at least the way that you're going to present the idea and the chains of belief, right, to move them to the point where they see that your, that your idea is really the only way to go about it. So your product is the only way that they should go. And you should also have your bucket named and that's the problem bucket that he talks about. And so you should have, um, and so, um, yep, sorry. So you have your story structure and then you have your bucket. Okay. So here's kind of that whole thing, uh, laid out in one big diagram. If you wanted to see that. So in order to kind of further consolidate this in my own mind, I have kind of developed this, um, this concept of the belief map. And if I follow these, these kind of steps, uh, I think it, you know, it helps me get to the point where I want to be. So the first thing I'm going to do is draw what my destination belief is. In that case, it's, um, you know, here I have it with this icon just symbolized as the X, but in the case of blue blockers, it was, these are the only sunglasses that are going to help, um, protect my eyes and they're, um, lightweight and they're sturdy and they're going to make everything more clear. In the case of Frank versus Matt, it is Matt is successful because he does the following things and Frank is not successful because he does the following things. And I am not yet successful because I've been acting like Matt, excuse me, like Frank and I need to act more like Matt. Then what we need to do is we need to determine a belief start point. This is based entirely on your understanding of the marketplace. So where, where is your ideal customer right now? Have they even tried it to make money online? Do they believe that they can make money online? Have they tried it, but they failed? If it's in weight loss, you know, do they already believe that, um, lifting weights is a better, better way to lose weight than cardio? Do they already believe that sugar is bad and they want to get rid of it or they don't know that sugar is bad? You know, where are they in the whole spectrum of knowledge that they have to have in order to agree with your position? Where do they first plot out? Okay. Then you're going to come in and you're just going to have the little belief steps, right? So I just have little steps here that show, um, the little logical belief systems that they need to believe in order to arrive at the point where we want them to arrive. Then, um, and this essentially, you know, this, um, this here was summarized in step number one up above. This really is all step number two as described above. And I have this idea of describe the symptoms and isolate the real underlying problem. I chose this little symbol of, um, kind of a tree and a root system and having the branches because Henry David Thoreau once said that thousands are hacking at the branches of evil while only one attacks the root. I like that quote, especially in reference to what we are trying to do with how we present this information. And um, that's the idea is that look, everybody else is kind of addressing the symptoms of the deeper of the bigger problem, but the real issue is down here at the root cause. And that's where we need to, that's what we need to identify. So you know, having this mindset that, um, you can beat the system and that there's some type of hack and that there's some type of, um, new way discovered to do something is actually symptoms of a bigger problem. That problem is called shiny object syndrome. And that's what you have Frank Frank, you chase shiny objects and you're going to keep chasing these shiny objects all over the place and you're never going to progress. Look at Matt over there who does not have shiny object syndrome because he instead of focusing on multiple things, focuses on one thing instead of chasing new hacks and tricks and techniques, he actually has been focusing on just learning a proven system. And that's all he's focused on for a year and looking how much farther along he is. Right. So that's kind of how all that would work. So if you look at this idea of the belief map, what you're going to do first is you first plot out your destination, then your start point based on your customer information and the knowledge that you have about your marketplace. And then you just connect the dots. What do I need people to believe to get to point eight to point B? And then we look at the symptoms that they have and then we identify the root cause and this root cause becomes your problem bucket. Right. So that's the idea of the problem bucket. That's what that is. Okay. So here's a little diagram you can use just fill in these blanks. So when you first map it, step one, you go right here and you just have your fill in the blanks to what your belief destination is. You know, just write it out. And then you're going to say, okay, what's something they have to believe before that? What's something they believe before that? And then what do I know about where my customer is actually starting from? Right. And so when we map everything out, we move this way backwards. When we actually begin to tell it, we move this way. And this really, the way that you do this is by just saying, I describe the current pain or the current situation. That's all we need to do right there in belief step number one and belief step number two. All we're going to do is make sure that we make a statement about what it is and then we give some proof. And so go back and read the blue blocker ad. Look at how he makes a statement and then he provides proof and look at Frank versus Matt. And the statement is essentially, you know, look at what Matt tried to do and look at the result, which is the proof. And then look at what Frank tried to do and look at the result and that's the proof. And whether that was every decision, every decision that Matt made, right? So if it was the decision to settle on one particular way of making money online and look at the result versus Frank who chose likely multiple ways to try to make money online and didn't really stick with any of them. When you get to this level and you start identifying, you know, what belief step two and three is and you kind of look at how people that when you look at, when you look at people in your marketplace that don't adhere to believing in whatever, you know, belief step two and belief step three is for you, you know, what are the characteristics that they have when they don't follow your advice, when they, when they violate this kind of principle that you have about how you deliver something and then start naming that bucket, right? So you name that problem bucket. Then when you have your belief destination kind of mapped out, all you do is just describe how good life is now, right? So it's just kind of a before and after view between their start point all the way over to where you're describing what life would be like if you arrive at that belief destination.
